responses: We built an AI system for automated regulatory document analysis at a financial services firm. The system reads regulatory filings, guidance documents, and enforcement actions from 14 regulatory bodies and identifies changes that require action from our compliance, legal, or product teams. When a relevant change is detected, the system generates an impact assessment specifying which business lines, products, and controls are affected, the required action category (policy update, control modification, regulatory filing, or monitoring enhancement), a proposed response timeline based on effective dates, and a confidence rating for each finding. The problem solved is that our regulatory change management process was consuming 800+ analyst-hours per month and still missing approximately 12% of material changes, as measured by a retrospective audit of enforcement actions against peer firms. Post-deployment over 22 months, analyst time on regulatory monitoring has dropped to 180 hours per month, and our retrospective miss rate has dropped to 1.4%, maintained over a continuous 12-month measurement window. The system has also increased the lead time we have to respond to regulatory changes: average lead time from effective date to completed action has improved from 8 days to 34 days.

This was our third attempt to automate regulatory change management — the first two are instructive about why the architecture we chose is the one that works. The first attempt used keyword monitoring with pre-defined rule sets — it was fast and auditable but generated 40x more alerts than were material, because regulatory language reuses common terms across different contexts. Analyst alert fatigue caused the monitoring to be deprioritized informally, which was the condition that led to the 12% miss rate we were trying to solve. The second attempt used a fine-tuned classification model trained on our historical change log — it improved precision substantially but was completely blind to new regulatory domains that had not appeared in the training data, and those were precisely the areas where we were most likely to miss material changes. The third attempt — our current system — uses a three-stage pipeline. Stage 1 is a domain relevance classifier that filters the regulatory document universe to documents relevant to our business domains. Stage 2 is an impact assessment model that scores relevance of specific document sections to our business lines and controls. Stage 3 is an LLM synthesis layer that reads the high-scoring sections and generates the impact narrative. The explicit tradeoff we accepted: stages 1 and 2 are auditable and can be explained to regulators; stage 3 is not, which means we cannot use the LLM output as a primary reference in regulatory correspondence — it must be reviewed and re-stated by a compliance analyst before external communication. We also explicitly chose not to use the system for cross-jurisdictional conflict detection, because that problem requires reasoning about regulatory intent that we do not trust the system to handle reliably. This is a documented system boundary that is reviewed annually.

Our failure mode register has 12 items. I will cover the five highest-severity cases. First: the system misses a material regulatory change that leads to a compliance violation. This is the failure mode the entire system exists to prevent. Mitigation: we maintain a secondary keyword monitoring layer that runs independently and catches any document the primary system misses; once per quarter, a compliance analyst reviews a random sample of documents the primary system classified as non-material to verify the classification. Recovery: if a missed change leads to a regulatory inquiry, we have a prepared incident response protocol that includes disclosure to the regulatory body, root cause analysis, and a corrective action plan submitted within 30 days. Second: the LLM synthesis layer produces an incorrect impact assessment that causes a compliance team to take the wrong action or to miss a required action. Mitigation: impact assessments above a materiality threshold require an independent review by a second compliance analyst before the action plan is finalized; the analyst review must identify and document any disagreements with the AI assessment. Third: regulatory body changes its document format, causing stage 1 or stage 2 to fail silently — documents are processed but key sections are missed. Mitigation: format validation runs on every document; when a document structure deviates from the learned pattern by more than a threshold, it is flagged for manual review. Fourth: the system develops a systematic blind spot for changes from a specific regulatory body due to document formatting or language patterns that confuse the classifier. Mitigation: we track detection rates by regulatory body separately; any body with a materially lower detection rate triggers a model review. Fifth: the compliance analyst review step becomes a rubber stamp — analysts approve AI impact assessments without genuine independent analysis. Mitigation: analyst reviews are sampled monthly by the compliance manager, who compares the analyst's documented reasoning against the AI assessment; divergence rate below a floor triggers a review of whether the review process is functioning as designed.

My approach to AI override has evolved significantly over the 5 years I have worked on AI systems in regulated industries, and this project represents the most explicit version of the framework I use now. The central insight is that there are two distinct questions when working with AI output: is this output correct, and should this output be trusted for this decision? These are not the same question. An output can be correct and still not be trustworthy for the decision — because the decision requires a level of certainty or an audit trail that the AI cannot provide. Conversely, an output might have a known error rate that is acceptable for the decision. I apply this distinction to every AI component we build. For stage 1 and stage 2 of our system, the outputs are trusted for the decision (routing and scoring) because they are auditable, have known error rates measured against ground truth, and the consequences of errors are recoverable. For stage 3, the outputs are not trusted for any external decision without human re-statement — regardless of confidence score — because we cannot audit the reasoning and the consequences of errors in regulatory correspondence are not recoverable. This distinction is documented in our system governance policy and reviewed with every new team member. Specific incidents that shaped this framework: the LLM synthesis layer produced a confident and well-written impact assessment for a regulatory change that cited the correct regulatory section but inverted the requirement — describing a restriction as a permission. This was caught by the compliance analyst review. After root cause analysis, we found the model was applying a common reading of similar-sounding language from a different jurisdiction and importing that interpretation. We added a jurisdiction isolation step that prevents the synthesis model from applying cross-jurisdictional precedent without explicit flagging. The incident also caused us to permanently remove the confidence score display from stage-3 outputs, because confidence scores were creating an anchoring effect that made analysts less likely to challenge high-confidence outputs.

rationale: High L5. All dimensions at 5 except override evidence at 4. Override evidence earns 4 because a documented decision framework for trust vs correctness exists and specific incidents shaped it, but the framework was developed through accumulated experience rather than being a pre-designed systematic approach applied from the start.
